content:
OpenAI released the GPT-5.6 family on July 9, 2026. Here’s what actually changed — with numbers.

10 min read
Just now

--

Most “what’s new in GPT-X” articles are written by people who read the announcement blog post and called it research, and I say this as someone who has written that kind of article before — you read the headline numbers, look at the benchmark table, note which numbers went up and by how much, and call that an analysis of what actually changed, when the question of whether any of it changes how you should use the tool is a different question that most of those articles don’t attempt to answer.
Last Tuesday I was on the metro between Saket and Mandi House with the OpenAI system card, three independent evaluations, and the primary pricing documentation open simultaneously on my phone — which is a lot of reading for a commute, and I had to go through the Nerova long-context evaluation twice before I understood why Luna’s numbers looked so different from Sol and Terra — but what came out of that reading was a more specific picture than what I’d seen in the coverage, and specifically a clearer answer to the question I actually wanted answered: of everything that changed between GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.6, which changes are real, which are marketing, and which should actually change something about how I work.
